logline: When the old gods devour their own children out of fear, one hidden child becomes the vengeful force that remakes the divine order forever.
premise: Most retellings of the Clash of the Titans focus on spectacular battles, but the real truth at the heart of the Theogony is family betrayal so deep it shakes the cosmos. Cronus consumes his children, haunted by the prophecy that one will overthrow him—yet the act of devouring is what ensures his doom. Zeus's uprising is less a hero's quest and more a cycle of violence passed on from parent to child, birthing a new world stained by old wounds.
narrative_arc: In the shadow of his father's paranoia, Cronus rules by consuming his newborns, trying to outmaneuver fate. Yet Rhea secretly saves Zeus, raising him in exile while Cronus grows ever more haunted and tyrannical—until Zeus returns, igniting a war that tears apart the heavens and the earth. In the aftermath, Zeus claims the throne, but the victory feels hollow—a traumatized order built on rebellion and fear, with the seeds of yet another cycle sown.
central_conflict: The desperate struggle between generations, as power is seized through betrayal and paranoia, while each act of violence ensures its own repetition.
emotional_journey: Opening with a sense of inherited dread—the suffocating fear of inevitable betrayal—and ending with uneasy triumph, where victory tastes of loss and the old terror is simply reborn in new hands.
core_message: The greatest rebellions are born from the wounds inflicted by those meant to protect us, and the price of seizing power is carrying forward the scars of those who came before.
unique_angle: This version rejects the glamour of divine war and instead frames the gods’ conflict as a generational trauma—where every act of rebellion is a response to parental violence, and every victory carries the echo of the past.
